_comment: Breadth pool: open-ended enumeration questions with a ground-truth RUBRIC (set of distinct valid points from canonical checklists). breadth_probe.sh measures arm-A coverage = fraction of rubric a single strong model hits. ~100% -> breadth saturates; partial -> headroom, run the panel union. [rubrics DEEPENED 2026-07-22 with niche points so W cannot hit the ceiling]

questions.prompt:
You are security-reviewing this authentication design. Enumerate EVERYTHING that could go wrong — list every distinct security risk you can identify.

DESIGN: A web app authenticates users by issuing a JWT signed with HS256 using a shared secret string. The token payload is {user_id, role}. On each request the server verifies the JWT signature (using the algorithm named in the token header) and reads `role` to authorize actions. The token is stored in the browser's localStorage and sent in the Authorization header. There is no separate session store.
rubric: algorithm confusion - server trusts the alg in the token header (alg=none accepted, or RS256->HS256 downgrade using the public key as the HMAC secret)
no token expiration (exp) - tokens are valid forever
no revocation/logout - a stolen or compromised token cannot be invalidated (no session store)
weak/guessable HMAC shared secret enables offline brute-force and token forgery
token in localStorage is exfiltratable by any XSS (should be an httpOnly cookie)
authorization trusts the role claim in the token - privilege escalation if the token is forgeable or the role is client-influenced
no audience/issuer (aud/iss) binding - a token is replayable across services or environments
the JWT payload is only base64, not encrypted - user_id and role are readable by anyone holding the token
no replay or channel binding - a captured token can be reused (no device/session/DPoP binding)
no signing-key rotation strategy
transport not stated - without enforced TLS the bearer token is interceptable on the wire
kid header injection - attacker manipulates the key-lookup (path traversal or SQL injection via kid) to control the verification key
jku/x5u or embedded JWK header trusted - the verifier could fetch an attacker-supplied key if any asymmetric path is enabled
no nbf/iat validation and no clock-skew/leeway policy - boundary acceptance of not-yet-valid or just-expired tokens
no typ/token-type enforcement - an id or refresh token accepted where an access token is expected (token confusion)
stale privileges - a role baked into a long-lived token cannot be downgraded after a permission change without a server-side re-check
no refresh-token rotation or max session lifetime - a leaked long-lived token is durable
token leakage via access logs, proxies, or Referer - the Authorization header or a token-in-URL captured downstream
non-constant-time signature or secret comparison - timing side-channel
denial of service via an oversized or deeply-nested token - parser/verify resource exhaustion
one shared secret authorizes every endpoint and service - no scoping, so a single leak forges tokens everywhere

questions.prompt:
You are reviewing this webhook-delivery system for problems. Enumerate EVERYTHING that could go wrong — list every distinct risk, failure mode, or abuse you can identify.

DESIGN: A SaaS delivers event notifications to customer-provided webhook URLs via HTTP POST with a JSON body. Customers register any URL. On a 2xx response the event is marked delivered; on any failure the system retries immediately, repeatedly, until it succeeds. Delivery is handled by a pool of workers pulling from a single queue.
rubric: SSRF - a customer URL can target internal services or the cloud metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254)
no request signing/HMAC - the receiver cannot verify authenticity; payloads are spoofable
retries without an idempotency key - duplicate deliveries; the receiver double-processes
immediate and unbounded retries - a retry storm / self-DDoS against a failing endpoint (needs exponential backoff)
no max-retry cap or dead-letter - a permanently-failing (poison) endpoint retries forever and blocks the queue
no per-request timeout - a slow or hanging endpoint exhausts the worker pool
no ordering guarantee - events can arrive out of order
sensitive event data is sent to an arbitrary third-party URL (data exposure)
no backpressure - queue growth under sustained failure
DNS rebinding / TOCTOU - the URL is validated once but resolves differently at delivery time
no per-destination rate limiting, delivery observability, or replay for customers
no signature timestamp+nonce - a captured delivery can be replayed against the receiver
no TLS certificate verification on the outbound call - MITM/interception of the delivery
follows HTTP redirects - a 3xx to an internal address bypasses URL allowlisting (SSRF)
IPv6, link-local, or decimal/octal IP encodings bypass naive URL validation
no response size or read cap - a malicious endpoint streams unbounded data and exhausts the worker
single shared queue - one customer's failures starve all others (no per-tenant isolation or fairness)
no jitter on recovery - synchronized retries thundering-herd the endpoint when it returns
a 2xx does not mean processed - the receiver can return 200 then drop the event, silent loss with no reconciliation
no re-validation of the URL over time - allowed at registration, later repointed to an internal address
no signing-key rotation or key-distribution mechanism to customers

questions.prompt:
You are reviewing this caching layer for problems. Enumerate EVERYTHING that could go wrong — list every distinct failure mode or correctness/reliability risk you can identify.

DESIGN: A read-heavy service uses cache-aside with Redis in front of Postgres. On read: look up the key in Redis; on a miss, query Postgres, write the result into Redis with a 1-hour TTL, and return it. On write: update the row in Postgres, then delete the key from Redis. TTL is fixed at 3600s for every key.
rubric: cache stampede / thundering herd - many concurrent misses on a hot key all hit Postgres at once (no single-flight or lock)
write race - update-then-delete versus a concurrent read-then-set can leave a STALE value cached until the TTL expires
no negative caching - repeated misses for nonexistent keys hammer Postgres (cache penetration)
fixed 3600s TTL for every key - synchronized mass expiry causes periodic load spikes (no TTL jitter)
delete-after-write is not atomic with the DB commit - a crash between commit and delete leaves stale cache
Redis unavailability behavior is unspecified - fail-open (DB load spike) versus fail-closed (outage)
no eviction or memory policy - memory pressure evicts hot keys unpredictably
read-your-writes / replica lag - a read from a Postgres replica after a write can repopulate a stale value
hot-key or large-value skew overloads a single Redis node
no serialization versioning - a schema change makes cached blobs unreadable across deploys
unbounded key cardinality - caching everything with a 1h TTL blows up Redis memory
cache avalanche - a Redis restart or flush drops all keys at once and overwhelms Postgres (no warmup or staggering)
a delete failure is not retried - a transient Redis error during the write path silently leaves stale data until the TTL
no hit-rate or staleness monitoring - silent degradation to the DB goes unnoticed
Redis is a single point of failure - no replication, so a failover loses the cache and causes a load spike
TTL too long for volatile data changed by another writer or path that does not delete the key - stale reads within the hour
two concurrent misses both populate - last-writer-wins may persist a value from an older DB snapshot
attacker-influenced key derivation - key collision or poisoning serves the wrong tenant's data
